original_abstract: From the Arab Spring to more recent protest movements in the EU, Russia, and Turkey, modern uprisings are frequently characterized as "Twitter revolutions." This label highlights the decisive role of new media, the speed of international coverage, and the resulting cycle of global reactions. Yet, this dynamic is not entirely modern; with the rise of the printing press, early-modern revolts faced a remarkably similar media revolution. The introduction of print fundamentally altered the trajectory of these conflicts, which often escalated into full-scale propaganda wars once insurgents gained access to the press. Beyond the battlefield, this shift also transformed the way revolts were narrated. Because domestic governments strictly censored and persecuted dissident views, local accounts were often stifled. In contrast, observers across borders—free from such local allegiances—were able to reflect more openly on these events. Consequently, this book examines how revolts were represented and reimagined across international boundaries, where the absence of censorship allowed for more complex interpretations.
humanized_abstract: THE ARAB SPRING, more recent waves of civil unrest in various EU, Russian and Turkish cities, make us increasingly talk about "Twitter revolutions" (Dahl 2012, McFadden 2011). Whatever the causal relationship between revolts and social media (Von Sierkhan 2013), "Twitter revolutions" imply the use of new media technologies, immediate global coverage, and consequently, the acceleration of the round-the-world reaction loop. These revolutionary features, however, are not unprecedented. In his seminal work Media of Annihilation, Casper Eric Jao demonstrates that "Twitter revolutions" have an early-modern counterpart: The printing press caused early-modern rebellions to undergo an unprecedented change as the rebels aspired to engage in a propaganda war. When we look beyond the fighting, we find that the discourse of rebellions underwent a crucial transformation. Since governments could do whatever it took to prohibit and punish discussions of antigovernmental nature at home, no one was allowed to freely discuss the motivations and goals of rebellions in their own countries. On the other hand, foreign observers, being devoid of any indigenous stake or attachment, were generally permitted to share more nuanced insights about events unfolding abroad. Hence, for the first time in history, a revolt, this book scrutinizes the international representation of revolts, focusing on the transborder aspects of discourses where "free from local constraint(s), foreign contemporaneous accounts provide a more diverse and multifaceted representation of the rebellions than ever before.
